video_title: The SpaceX IPO, Fable 5, AI Capex Update & Market Check w/ Gavin Baker, Andrew Fox & Clark Tang
gist: Gerstner, Baker, Fox and Tang argue the SpaceX IPO (priced ~$135/share, ~$1.77T, IPO in 2 days) is a 'must-own' bet on both space and AI, framing SpaceX as a top-4 AI hyperscaler ('Elon Web Services') almost overnight after Anthropic/Google compute deals plus the Cursor/xAI model. They contend AI inference revenue and per-gigawatt monetization are rising faster than consensus, validating ~$1.5T/yr capex by 2027, and that frontier closed models keep capturing ~90% of economic value even as open source dominates token volume.

insights.claim: SpaceX is IPO'ing at $135/share, a ~$1.77T valuation, with Goldman/WSJ models projecting ~$160B revenue in 2028 versus roughly $18B last year, implying an ~8x revenue ramp over 3-4 years.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Sets the bar: the bull case requires near-unprecedented revenue compounding, so each business line (Starlink, terrestrial AI compute, model) must independently clear high hurdles.
insights.quote: we know that the IPO is $135 a share 1.77 trillion

insights.claim: The two biggest swing variables for SpaceX value are how fast they bring on terrestrial data centers and whether the Cursor-trained model reaches the Pareto frontier, not orbital compute.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Tells investors where to focus diligence and what data releases (e.g., Grok 4.3 + Cursor model) will move the valuation.

insights.claim: After the Anthropic and Google compute deals, SpaceX's valuation fell from ~100x trailing TTM revenue to roughly 39x, because they added about $29B of revenue in a single month.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: The multiple compresses as deals are signed, so headline 'expensive' framing may understate how quickly contracted revenue re-rates the stock.
insights.quote: it was a hundred times trailing TTM revenue. Well, after the deals they signed, I think it's 39 times.

insights.claim: SpaceX is positioned as a 'must buy, must own, set it and forget it' core institutional holding as the single best bet on both the space and AI future.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Frames the IPO as a long-term anchor position rather than a trade, signaling large sponsor demand at the offering.
insights.quote: for most institutional investors, it's a must buy, a must own. It's set it and forget it

insights.claim: Total AI capital being raised across Anthropic, OpenAI and SpaceX is roughly $250B, only about 1% of the Mag 7 market cap.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Reframes 'bubble' fears: the incremental private AI capital is small relative to public mega-cap value, supporting continued funding.
insights.quote: let's call it $250 billion. That's 1% of the mag 7.

insights.claim: Rapid two-stage reusability of Starship is the single hardest gating condition for SpaceX's economics; SpaceX plans to attempt second-stage recovery later this year and refly it next year.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Reusability milestones are the key technical catalysts to track; failure delays Starlink V3, direct-to-cell and orbital compute.
insights.quote: rapid reusability is the main thing that we're watching for

insights.claim: SpaceX is scaling from ~160-165 launches last year toward high-hundreds within several years and into the thousands within three years thereafter, implying two-to-three launches per day.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Launch cadence is the throughput constraint enabling all downstream revenue; cadence misses cap the model.
insights.quote: getting into the thousands of launches probably in the next three years thereafter

insights.claim: Two-stage reusability could cut launch cost from ~$1,500/kg on Falcon toward ~$250/kg and eventually well below as cost asymptotes to fuel.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: The cost-per-kg curve is what makes orbital compute viable; quantifies the economic unlock from reusability.
insights.quote: going from $1,500 per kg on Falcon, somewhere in that range to 250

insights.claim: Starlink broadband has penetrated less than 1% of global households and could scale to hundreds of millions of terminals, with connectivity revenue modeled to grow from ~$10B to ~$50B by 2028.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: $50B is only ~0.3% of the global telecom market, framing the connectivity ramp as conservative and achievable.
insights.quote: You look at the percent of global households with Starlink, it's less than 1%.

insights.claim: Starlink wins on better/faster/cheaper: lowest latency and fastest connection globally today, and likely cheapest cost per gigabyte once rapid reusability arrives.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: A durable product-advantage argument for taking telecom share, supporting the direct-to-cell growth thesis.
insights.quote: better, faster, cheaper has been a winning formula

themes.theme: Elon Web Services: SpaceX as AI hyperscaler

insights.claim: SpaceX became the number-four AI hyperscaler within roughly 30 days, passing Oracle, after striking compute deals with Anthropic and Google ('Elon Web Services').
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: A brand-new, fast-growing revenue line most pre-IPO models omitted; potential consolidation winner in AI compute.
insights.quote: In 30 days, we went from not being an AI hyperscaler to being number four. And we passed a lot of companies, including Oracle.

insights.claim: xAI's compute deals generate more operating profit per gigawatt than Anthropic, Meta, Google or OpenAI, with Colossus 1 estimated at a 55% IRR versus 6-8% borrowing costs.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: If accurate, the spread between cost of capital and returns is enormous, justifying aggressive debt-financed buildout.
insights.quote: if you can borrow money at six seven 8% and invest in something with a 55% ARR... that math maths

insights.claim: Elon's team stood up a 100,000-GPU cluster in ~19 days and builds data centers in ~122 days versus an industry norm of ~3 years to plan plus ~1 year to commission, making him an 'N of one'.
insights.speaker: Clark Tang
insights.so_what: Speed is cost and revenue: faster energization means earlier monetization and a structural edge over NeoCloud rivals.
insights.quote: We're talking about 19 days.

insights.claim: Data centers are not commodities; Elon re-engineered data-center design from first principles, creating a differentiated, hard-to-replicate advantage that enables consolidation of the AI compute market.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Counters the bear view that compute is commoditized; supports durable margins and pricing power for SpaceX compute.
insights.quote: I think there is a belief that these data centers are commodities... And I do not share that belief.

insights.claim: Google is paying SpaceX a premium for terrestrial compute partly as a call option to be first in line for future space-based compute.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Explains the pricing premium and signals strategic scarcity value of SpaceX's orbital roadmap to hyperscalers.
insights.quote: pay a little call option to get first in line for space

insights.claim: xAI/SpaceX has reportedly secured up to ~20% of Vera Rubin capacity, with overprocured scarce GPU capacity monetizable at best-in-class margins even if their own frontier model effort stumbles.
insights.speaker: Clark Tang
insights.so_what: Downside protection: scarce chip allocation can be resold profitably, de-risking the compute bet.
insights.quote: it looks like they've, you know, secured maybe up to 20% of Vera Rubin capacity

insights.claim: Orbital compute is not required for the SpaceX IPO valuation but is a meaningful call option on top of it.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Investors can underwrite the deal on terrestrial economics alone, treating space as optionality rather than a required leap of faith.
insights.quote: I don't think orbital compute is is necessary for the IPO valuation, but it's certainly important

insights.claim: Putting a gigawatt of compute in space could cost ~$5B in launch capex (≈5 MW per Starship, ~100 metric tons) versus $20-25B terrestrially for power/shell/cooling, a ~5x reduction on half the data-center bill of materials.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Quantifies the orbital cost advantage; if reliability holds, space compute could undercut terrestrial on the non-GPU cost stack.
insights.quote: the math you get to is it's about $5 billion per gigawatt of capex to put these in space

insights.claim: A terrestrial gigawatt costs ~$60B today (~$35B GPUs/silicon, ~$25B land/shell/power/cooling), with the non-GPU portion likely inflationary while space power and cooling are effectively free.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Rising terrestrial infra costs plus near-free space resources widen the orbital advantage over time, contingent on satellite reliability.
insights.quote: it cost $60 billion to put a gigawatt on the ground today

insights.claim: Orbital compute viability hinges on satellite/GPU reliability in space, since GPUs melt and lasers fail; as long as in-space failure rates aren't dramatically worse, 'the math maths' once Starship reusability arrives.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Identifies reliability/maintenance as the key remaining technical risk to the orbital thesis.
insights.quote: as long as these satellites in space aren't failing at an a at an astronomical rate the math maths

themes.theme: xAI model & Cursor as overlooked upside

insights.claim: The most underappreciated source of SpaceX upside is the model business, supercharged by acquiring Cursor (~700-800 people, projected up to ~$10B revenue exit this year) and training it on xAI's massive compute.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: If the model line scales from ~$10B toward a large share of $150B AI revenue, it is the biggest upside surprise candidate in the IPO.
insights.quote: this is the place that's getting the least amount of attention and could have the biggest upside surprise

insights.claim: Cursor and Anthropic each reportedly hold more proprietary coding tokens than exist on the public internet, and Cursor's Composer 2.5 became Pareto-dominant after just three weeks training on Colossus 2.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Proprietary coding data plus compute is a moat; explains why xAI could rapidly become a real coding-model contender.
insights.quote: each have more tokens of proprietary coding data than exist on the public internet

insights.claim: Coding may be the fastest path to AGI/ASI ('bitter lesson adjacent') because a model good at coding can write code to do anything.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Elevates coding-model leadership as strategically central, not just a vertical; supports outsized value for Cursor/Anthropic.
insights.quote: coding may be the fastest path to AGI and ASI because if you're really good at coding you can write code

insights.claim: xAI's Grok 4.3 (1.5T-parameter, ~500B active) was on the Pareto frontier ~12 days ago; injecting Cursor data into pre-training (not just RL) is the key upcoming data point.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Watch the next Grok+Cursor release as a catalyst confirming whether xAI is a durable frontier player.
insights.quote: most intelligent 500 billion parameter model in the world

insights.claim: Only four companies are on the AI frontier: xAI/SpaceX, Google (Gemini 3.1 Pro), Anthropic and OpenAI, with Anthropic and OpenAI dominating.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Defines the competitive set for frontier-model value capture; membership shifts quickly with each release.
insights.quote: there are four companies on the frontier XAI SpaceX AAI Google one with Gemini 3.1 Pro and then the rest of it was dominated by anthropic and open AI

insights.claim: We may never know how smart frontier models actually are because no one runs them continuously long enough (e.g., a model thinking for a year) before the next generation ships.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Implies massive latent capability and demand for sustained inference compute, reinforcing the bullish compute thesis.
insights.quote: nobody has run mythos for a year continuously and we may never know how smart each generation of models actually is

insights.claim: Snapshot benchmarks are becoming obsolete; the relevant axis for model capability is now time/tokens/compute since frontier models can solve most problems if allowed to run long enough.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Reframes how to evaluate models and underwrites unbounded inference-compute demand 'as far as the eye can see'.
insights.quote: the X-axis has to be time or tokens or compute

insights.claim: Frontier closed models capture roughly 90% of AI economic value while open source represents ~80% of tokens consumed, decisively refuting the thesis that cheap open-source tokens would erode frontier revenue.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Value accrues to the frontier even as volume goes open source; supports premium pricing power for leading labs.
insights.quote: frontier might be 90% of the econ economic value right open source might be 80% of tokens

insights.claim: The better open source does, the more bullish it is for compute/hardware, because frontier labs capturing less margin means more total spend flows to compute providers.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Open-source success is a tailwind for compute names, not a bear case; useful for sizing semis vs. model-lab exposure.
insights.quote: It's actually really bullish for compute and hardware because if the frontier models are capturing less of the margin, then you're going to spend more on compute.

insights.claim: Fable 5 (Anthropic, essentially Mythos with safety classifiers that fail back to Opus 4.8) excels at multi-agent orchestration and long-running tasks, enabling work like refactoring a 50M-line Ruby codebase at Stripe in a day.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Concrete productivity step-change drives enterprise adoption and token consumption, validating the demand side.
insights.quote: 50 million line Ruby code base at Stripe that was you know uh refactored in a day versus many weeks with many people

insights.claim: Harvey used proprietary legal data plus RL/fine-tuning on an open-source model with a router to beat Opus 4.7/4.8 outcomes at lower cost, illustrating the routing-based future where most tokens are open source but high-value queries stay frontier.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Model routing is the emerging enterprise architecture; signals where margin pools (frontier vs open) settle by workload.

themes.theme: Nvidia, ASICs & the compute supply chain

insights.claim: Nvidia has held or even gained market share against ASICs over recent years, contradicting consensus expectations of dramatic share loss on revenue, gigawatt and unit bases.
insights.speaker: Clark Tang
insights.so_what: Undercuts the ASIC-displacement bear case for Nvidia; supports continued Nvidia revenue dominance.
insights.quote: everyone assumed that Nvidia was going to lose share dramatically... and actually if you actually look at the last few years you know they've actually maintained their share very very handsomely

insights.claim: In a watt-constrained world, Nvidia's higher tokens-per-watt means more revenue per factory; alternative chips may save capex but yield less revenue and lower margins, a point Jensen keeps hammering.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Reframes chip choice as revenue-maximization not cost-minimization, favoring Nvidia while power is the binding constraint.
insights.quote: If you can get more tokens per watt, which is literally revenue with Nvidia, than a lot of alternatives

insights.claim: Among the OpenAI gigawatt allocations (Nvidia 10, Broadcom 10, AMD 6 with warrants, Cerebras ~1), the implied ~30% non-Nvidia share is very unlikely to be where deployments actually land.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Paper allocations overstate ASIC penetration; expect Nvidia to over-deliver versus stated splits.
insights.quote: I'll be very surprised if that is where they land.

insights.claim: Meta and Microsoft ASICs have been disappointing while OpenAI's 'Jalapeno' is a genuinely good chip, though it runs at lower temperature, requiring more cooling and power.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Differentiates ASIC winners/losers; Meta/Microsoft custom-silicon weakness is relevant to their AI cost structures.
insights.quote: Meta and Microsoft have been probably disappointing

insights.claim: Nvidia is highly likely to become the world's dominant provider of open-source AI and could join the frontier and become a top cloud-computing company faster than people think, especially if customers compete with it.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: A strategic option that could pressure ASIC economics and reposition Nvidia up the stack against its own customers.
insights.quote: Nvidia can join the frontier and become one of the world's largest cloud computing companies much faster than people think

insights.claim: Speed of energizing GPUs makes every supplier (power, land, turbines, generators) money faster, so vendors like GE Vernova will prefer reliable fast builders like xAI over the ~50 startup NeoClouds being funded.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Supply-chain preference compounds the advantage of fast builders, squeezing under-capitalized NeoCloud entrants.
insights.quote: literally speed is money for all of the suppliers

themes.theme: AI capex vs revenue & the ROI debate

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley raised its 2027 AI capex forecast from $950B to $1.1T; including SpaceX/CoreWeave the panel pegs 2027 capex closer to $1.5T.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Sets the spend side of the ROI equation; a key figure for gauging whether inference revenue can justify the buildout.
insights.quote: upped their 2027 capex forecast from 950 billion to 1.1 trillion

insights.claim: AI inference revenue likely ends this year well over $200B and reaches ~$300B+ next year, with ~50-70% gross margins, so $1.5T of capex against rising inference revenue 'maths'.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts the capex-overspend bear case; the trajectory toward trillions by ~2029 underwrites continued semis demand.
insights.quote: I think we end this year well over 200 billion in inference revenue. Well over.

insights.claim: Per-gigawatt AI monetization has risen from ~$20B at the start of the year toward $30-40B, with that increase flowing through as near-pure margin on a fixed-cost base.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Rising monetization per watt is the core bullish signal: demand is outstripping supply and improving unit economics.
insights.quote: from, you know, call it like 20 20 billion um in the in the best best of cases for at the beginning of the year to now like 30 to even pushing 40 per gigawatt

insights.claim: Capex decisions made in November 2025 may now be returning triple the expected ROI ('accidental profitability'), because labs wanted to spend far more but were supply-constrained.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Explains the surprise positive economics and why labs keep flooring the accelerator despite prisoner's-dilemma risk.
insights.quote: You may be getting triple that return today.

insights.claim: Less than 0.2% of people on Earth currently use AI in an agentic way, implying a massive runway and a sustained compute shortage as adoption scales.
insights.speaker: Andrew Fox
insights.so_what: Tiny current penetration on a high-burn user base argues demand and pricing stay elevated, supporting the bull thesis.
insights.quote: Less than 0.2% of people on Earth are actually using AI in an agentic way.

insights.claim: Token pricing and compute costs are proving inflationary rather than smoothly deflationary this year, as demand outstrips supply and monetization per watt rises.
insights.speaker: Clark Tang
insights.so_what: Favors owning asset-heavy compute businesses; contradicts the consensus deflation narrative going into the year.
insights.quote: you want to own asset heavy businesses and inflationary environments and token pricing is going up

insights.claim: The average max drawdown post-IPO across ~20 large names (Facebook, Twitter, Alibaba, Shopify) exceeds 50%, and SpaceX's IPO is unprecedented in size and speed of index inclusion, so near-term volatility is likely.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations for IPO volatility; supports a 'set a base position, trade the ballast' approach.
insights.quote: the average max draw down post IPO for like 20 companies... is, you know, over 50%

insights.claim: Selling pressure at the SpaceX IPO may be limited: Elon owns ~50% and is locked up ~365 days, employees/investors have had ~20 semiannual liquidity windows over a decade yet chose to hold, and dribble-out share release is constrained until after first earnings.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker
insights.so_what: Limited float and committed holders could support the stock post-IPO despite the historical drawdown base rate.
insights.quote: he's locked up for 365 days or 366 days. So, we know he's not selling

insights.claim: 2026 shows huge dispersion: semis ripped (doubles and triples) while internet is down ~16% and software down ~8%, with index gains driven mainly by AI/compute components.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Performance concentrated in AI infrastructure; non-AI tech has lagged, guiding sector positioning.
insights.quote: semis rip this year... Internet's down 16%. Uh software is down 8% on the year.

insights.claim: Altimeter dialed exposure from 'large' down to 'medium/small' expecting a consolidation period after the sharp April/May run, citing higher expectations, geopolitics (Iran war, ~$100 oil) and inflation (CPI ~4.2%).
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: A near-term caution flag from a major AI bull; signals a possible breather before higher highs.
insights.quote: we dialed back from what I would call large for altimter to something kind of like medium small

insights.claim: Without Anthropic's revenue showing up this year (the market's key overhang), the whole market could have been down in 2026.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Identifies Anthropic revenue as the single most important market catalyst; its trajectory is a key macro signal to monitor.
insights.quote: if the anthropic revenue had not shown up this year because that was the overhang on the market, I think the whole market could be down this year

insights.claim: AI demand has been seasonally weak the last three summers as college students (heavy AI users) leave school, and a recent two-week shift toward cheaper open-source tokens (per the Silicon Data index) is being misread as bearish.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Warns investors not to overreact to seasonal token-consumption dips or open-source mix shifts as structural weakness.
insights.quote: AI has actually been seasonal for the last three summers.

insights.claim: The Mag 7 added ~$1T of revenue in the last seven years (versus 20+ years to reach the first $1T), adding ~$17T in market cap; SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI are forecast to add another $1T of revenue in 4-5 years.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: Quantifies the accelerating pace of value creation, underpinning the 'higher highs' long-term thesis through volatility.
insights.quote: we've added 1 trillion of revenue to the MAG 7 in the last seven years

insights.claim: AI is expected to transform 5-15% of global GDP (~$10T at 10%), framing the addressable prize that justifies riding through near-term market bumps.
insights.speaker: Brad Gerstner
insights.so_what: The TAM framing is the core long-duration bull case anchoring conviction despite drawdown risk.
insights.quote: This is going to transform 5 10 15% of global GDP... 10% of global GDP is 10 trillion.
